title: Apple Slices & Almond Butter
description: Simple, satisfying, and portable. Two tablespoons of almond butter with a sliced apple. The 3PM savior.
ingredients: 1 medium apple (Honeycrisp or Fuji work great)
steps: Wash and slice apple into wedges.
Serve with almond butter for dipping.
Optional: sprinkle cinnamon on top or drizzle with a tiny bit of honey.

title: Chicken & Rice Power Bowl
description: Grilled chicken, brown rice, black beans, avocado, and salsa. Meal prep king — makes 4 at once.
ingredients: 1 can (15 oz) black beans, drained and rinsed
steps: Cook brown rice according to package directions.
Season chicken with cumin, garlic powder, salt, and pepper.
Grill or pan-sear chicken for 6-7 minutes per side until cooked through (165°F internal).
Let chicken rest 5 minutes, then slice into strips.
Divide rice between 4 bowls or containers.
Top each with chicken, black beans, sliced avocado, and salsa.
Squeeze lime over each bowl. Refrigerate meal prep portions for up to 4 days.

title: Classic Sourdough at Elevation
description: A homemade sourdough recipe tuned for high altitude. 24-hour process, four simple ingredients, and a crackling crust that makes every slice worth the wait.
ingredients: Rice flour for dusting the proofing basket
steps: Feed your starter first thing in the morning. Use a 1:1:1 ratio of starter, flour, and water. Let it sit at room temperature until it has at least doubled in size and looks domed and bubbly — this usually takes 4 to 6 hours depending on your kitchen temperature. A warm spot (75–80°F) speeds things up. The starter is ready when a small spoonful floats in water.

In a large mixing bowl, combine 500g flour and 400g water. Mix with your hands or a Danish dough whisk until no dry flour remains — the dough will look shaggy and rough, and that is exactly right. Cover the bowl with a damp towel or plastic wrap and let it rest (autolyse) for 45 minutes. This rest lets the flour fully hydrate and starts gluten development before you ever touch it again.

After the autolyse, add 125g of your peaked starter and 13g salt directly on top of the dough. Pinch and fold the starter and salt into the dough until fully incorporated — this takes about 3 to 4 minutes. The dough will feel shaggy at first but will start coming together. Squeeze it through your fingers rather than kneading to keep things even.

Perform a set of stretch and folds: wet your hand, grab one side of the dough, stretch it up and fold it over to the opposite side. Rotate the bowl 90 degrees and repeat. Do all four sides — that is one set. Repeat this every 15 to 30 minutes for a total of 4 sets. You will feel the dough tighten and become smoother with each round. This builds the gluten structure that gives sourdough its open crumb.

After the final stretch and fold, cover the bowl tightly with plastic wrap and leave it on the counter for bulk fermentation. At high altitude, fermentation can move faster due to lower air pressure, so start checking around 6 to 8 hours. The dough is ready when it has grown by roughly 50 percent, feels airy and jiggly, and you can see bubbles along the sides of the bowl.

Dust your countertop generously with rice flour. Gently turn the dough out — do not punch it down or deflate it. Use a bench scraper and your hands to pre-shape the dough into a round. Let it rest uncovered for 15 to 20 minutes to relax the gluten.

Shape the loaf by pulling the edges of the dough toward the center, then flipping it seam-side down. Using both hands, cup the dough and drag it gently toward you on the unfloured surface to build tension in the outer skin. The surface should feel taut and smooth — this tension is what gives you a good oven spring and that signature ear.

Dust a proofing basket (banneton) generously with rice flour. Place the shaped dough seam-side up into the basket. Cover tightly with plastic wrap so it does not dry out. Place the basket in the refrigerator for an overnight cold proof — 10 to 14 hours. The cold retard slows fermentation, develops deeper tangy flavor, and makes the dough much easier to score and handle the next morning.

The next morning, you have some flexibility on when to bake. Pulling it out anywhere between 10 and 14 hours works well. A shorter cold proof gives a milder flavor and more oven spring. A longer proof develops more sour tang and a slightly denser crumb. Find the window that matches the flavor and texture you prefer — this is where sourdough becomes your own.

Place your Dutch oven (lid on) into the oven and preheat to 500°F. Let it heat for at least 30 minutes so the pot is ripping hot — this is critical for the initial burst of steam that creates a crispy crust.

While the oven preheats, take the proofing basket out of the fridge. Invert the dough onto a sheet of parchment paper. Using a sharp razor blade or bread lame held at a 45-degree angle, score a single swift slash about a quarter-inch deep across the top of the loaf. A confident, quick motion works best — hesitation leads to dragging.

Carefully lift the dough (using the parchment as a sling) into the screaming-hot Dutch oven. Cover with the lid. Bake at 500°F for 25 minutes with the lid on. The trapped steam creates the blistered, crackly crust.

After 25 minutes, remove the lid and reduce the oven temperature to 475°F. Bake uncovered for another 10 to 15 minutes until the crust is deep golden brown — go darker than you think. A well-baked crust should sound hollow when you tap the bottom of the loaf.

Remove the loaf from the Dutch oven and place it on a wire cooling rack. This is the hardest part — let it cool for at least 1 to 2 hours before slicing. The inside is still cooking from residual heat, and cutting too early lets the steam escape, leaving you with a gummy interior. Trust the process.

title: Game Day Taco Dip
description: Layered taco dip on a big platter — refried beans, seasoned Greek yogurt, and all the toppings. The appetizer I grew up eating every time IU basketball was on. Serve it with chips and watch it disappear.
ingredients: 1 1/2 cups shredded cheddar cheese (or Mexican blend)
steps: Grab a large platter or big baking dish — you want surface area here, not depth. This is a dip, not a casserole.
Open the can of refried beans and spread them across the bottom of the platter in a thin, even layer. Think of it as the foundation. Use the back of a spoon to smooth it out.
In a bowl, mix the Greek yogurt with the entire packet of taco seasoning until it's fully combined. This is the secret layer — it tastes like sour cream but with way better macros.
Spread the seasoned Greek yogurt over the bean layer in an even coat. Don't stress about making it perfect. It's game day, not a cooking show.

Now the fun part — pile on the toppings. Layer the shredded cheese, diced tomatoes, green peppers, onions, sliced black olives, and shredded lettuce across the top. Add jalapeños and green onions if you want some kick.
Garnish with fresh cilantro if you've got it. Set out your favorite tortilla chips around the platter or in a bowl on the side.
Serve immediately or cover and refrigerate for up to a couple hours before game time. Dig in with chips and don't overthink it.

title: Grandma's Banana Bread
description: A handwritten recipe passed down from Grandma — four ripe bananas, a cup of chopped nuts, and an hour in the oven. The kind of banana bread that makes the whole house smell like home.
ingredients: 1 cup milk mixed with 2 tablespoons vinegar (buttermilk substitute)
3 cups flour, sifted with 1 teaspoon baking soda
steps: Preheat your oven to 350°F. Grease and flour two standard loaf pans.
Mash the bananas in a large bowl with a fork until mostly smooth — a few small chunks are fine.
Mix the milk and vinegar together in a small bowl or measuring cup and set it aside for a few minutes to curdle into a buttermilk substitute.
In a large mixing bowl, cream the softened butter and sugar together until combined.
Add the beaten eggs and mashed bananas to the butter-sugar mixture and stir until well mixed.
Sift the flour and baking soda together, then add to the wet ingredients alternately with the milk-vinegar mixture, stirring until just combined. Do not overmix.
Fold in the chopped walnuts. Or make it your own — skip the nuts entirely, swap in pecans, add a cup of chocolate chips, or mix in whatever sounds good to you. The batter handles it all.
Divide the batter evenly between the two prepared loaf pans.
Bake at 350°F for 1 hour, or until a toothpick inserted in the center comes out clean.
Let the loaves cool in the pans for 10 minutes, then turn out onto a wire rack to cool completely before slicing.

title: Lemon Herb Salmon & Asparagus
description: Salmon fillet with lemon, garlic, herbs, and roasted asparagus. Fancy-looking but dead simple to make.
ingredients: 1 lemon (half for juice, half for slices)
1 tsp dried oregano or Italian seasoning
Optional: 1 cup cooked quinoa per serving
steps: Preheat oven to 400°F (200°C).
Place asparagus on a sheet pan, drizzle with 1 tbsp olive oil, salt, and pepper.
Season salmon with garlic, oregano, salt, pepper, and a squeeze of lemon juice.
Place salmon on the pan with the asparagus. Top with lemon slices.
Drizzle salmon with remaining olive oil.
Bake for 12-15 minutes until salmon flakes easily and asparagus is tender.
Serve with cooked quinoa if you want extra carbs.

title: Loaded Egg & Veggie Scramble
description: Three eggs, spinach, peppers, and feta with whole wheat toast. The weekday morning staple.
steps: Heat olive oil in a non-stick pan over medium heat.
Add diced bell pepper and cook for 2 minutes until slightly softened.
Add spinach and stir until wilted, about 1 minute.
Crack eggs into the pan and scramble with the vegetables.
Cook until eggs are set but still slightly soft, about 2-3 minutes.
Top with crumbled feta, salt, and pepper.
Serve with whole wheat toast.

title: PB & Banana Protein Shake
description: Banana, peanut butter, protein powder, milk, and ice. Takes 3 minutes. Tastes like dessert. Post-workout go-to.
ingredients: 1 scoop vanilla or chocolate protein powder
steps: Add milk and peanut butter to blender first.
Add banana (frozen works great), protein powder, and ice.
Blend on high for 30-45 seconds until smooth.
Add honey if you want extra sweetness. Serve immediately.

title: Potato & Jalapeno Pizza
description: Sourdough pizza with thinly sliced potatoes, jalapeno olive oil, and a Greek yogurt finish. Crispy, creamy, and a little bit spicy — this one always surprises people.
ingredients: 200g active sourdough starter (100% hydration, fed and doubled)
20g olive oil (optional — makes the dough more tender)
3–4 medium Yukon Gold potatoes, sliced paper-thin (use a mandoline)
Jalapeno olive oil, enough to generously coat each pie
Flaky sea salt for the potatoes
Plain Greek yogurt (full fat) for finishing
Fresh chives, finely snipped, for finishing
steps: Feed your sourdough starter in the morning using a 1:1:1 ratio of starter, flour, and water. Let it sit at room temperature until it has at least doubled and looks bubbly and domed — usually 4 to 6 hours. It is ready when a small spoonful floats in water.
In a large bowl, combine 1000g flour and 700g water. Mix until no dry flour remains — it will be shaggy and rough. Cover with a damp towel and let it autolyse for 45 minutes to 1 hour.

Add 200g of your peaked starter, 20g salt, and 20g olive oil on top of the dough. Pinch and squeeze everything together until fully incorporated, about 3 to 4 minutes. It will be sticky and messy at first — that is normal.

Perform stretch and folds every 20 to 30 minutes for a total of 4 sets. Wet your hand, grab one side, stretch it up, fold it over. Rotate the bowl 90 degrees and repeat for all four sides. That is one set. The dough will get noticeably smoother and tighter with each round.

After the last set of folds, cover the bowl tightly and let it bulk ferment on the counter. Check around 6 to 8 hours — you are looking for roughly 50 percent growth, visible bubbles on the sides, and a jiggly, airy feel when you shake the bowl.

Divide the dough into 4 equal pieces (about 480g each). Shape each piece into a tight ball by pulling the edges to the center and pinching the seam. Flip seam-side down and use your hands to drag the ball toward you on an unfloured surface to build tension. Place each ball into a lightly oiled container or onto a lightly oiled sheet pan. Cover tightly and cold proof in the fridge overnight — 12 to 24 hours.

While the dough cold proofs, prep your potatoes. Using a mandoline, slice Yukon Golds paper-thin — you want them almost translucent. Toss the slices in a bowl with a drizzle of regular olive oil, flaky salt, and black pepper. Keep them covered in the fridge until you are ready to build the pizzas.

When you are ready to bake, pull the dough balls out of the fridge 1 to 2 hours before you plan to fire. Let them come to room temperature — they will be much easier to stretch when they are not cold.

If using an Ooni or other outdoor pizza oven, fire it up and let it preheat for at least 20 to 30 minutes. You want the stone around 800 to 900°F. If using a home oven, crank it as high as it goes (usually 500 to 550°F) with a pizza steel or stone on the middle rack and let it preheat for at least 45 minutes to an hour. A pizza steel works better than a stone in a home oven because it transfers heat faster.

Stretch the dough. Flour your hands and the counter generously. Press the dough ball flat, leaving a small rim around the edge for the crust. Pick it up and use your knuckles and gravity to stretch it into a round — rotate as you go. Do not use a rolling pin. If it springs back, let it rest for a couple minutes and try again. You are going for about 12 to 14 inches.

Drizzle jalapeno olive oil generously over the stretched dough — this is your sauce. Use the back of a spoon to spread it evenly, leaving the outer crust bare. Do not be shy with it. The oil is doing all the heavy lifting for flavor on the base.

Shingle the potato slices over the entire surface of the pizza, overlapping slightly. You want a single thin layer that covers the dough. Hit them with one more small drizzle of jalapeno olive oil and a pinch of flaky salt.

Launch the pizza. In the Ooni, bake for 60 to 90 seconds, rotating every 15 to 20 seconds with a turning peel so it chars evenly. The potatoes should be tender and starting to crisp at the edges, and the crust should be blistered and leopard-spotted. In a home oven, bake for 6 to 8 minutes on the preheated steel or stone, rotating halfway through. Broil for the last 60 to 90 seconds if you want extra color on top.

Pull the pizza and immediately finish it. Drop spoonfuls of cold Greek yogurt across the top — do not spread it, just dollop it on in 5 or 6 spots. The contrast between the hot pizza and cold yogurt is the whole point. Snip fresh chives generously over the top. Slice and serve right away.

title: Protein Pancakes
description: Oats, banana, egg whites, and protein powder. The kids love these too — weekend breakfast that doesn't derail you.
ingredients: Cooking spray or butter for pan
Toppings: berries, maple syrup, peanut butter
steps: Blend oats, banana, egg whites, protein powder, milk, baking powder, and vanilla in a blender until smooth.
Heat a non-stick pan or griddle over medium heat and spray with cooking spray.
Pour about 1/4 cup batter per pancake.
Cook until bubbles form on surface, about 2-3 minutes, then flip.
Cook another 1-2 minutes until golden.
Serve with berries, a drizzle of maple syrup, or a tablespoon of peanut butter.

title: Sheet Pan Chicken & Veggies
description: Everything on one pan. Chicken thighs, sweet potatoes, broccoli, olive oil. Minimal dishes, maximum flavor.
steps: Preheat oven to 425°F (220°C).
Cube sweet potatoes and toss with 1 tbsp olive oil, salt, and pepper on a large sheet pan.
Season chicken thighs with paprika, garlic powder, onion powder, salt, and pepper.
Nestle chicken on the pan with the sweet potatoes.
Roast for 20 minutes.
Add broccoli florets to the pan, drizzle with remaining olive oil.
Roast for another 12-15 minutes until chicken reaches 165°F and vegetables are tender.

title: Grandma's Snow Ice Cream
description: Fresh snow, milk, sugar, and vanilla. Four ingredients, zero cooking, and a whole lot of childhood memories. The original snow day tradition.
ingredients: 8 cups fresh, clean snow (the fluffy white stuff — not the stuff by the road)
1 cup whole milk (or half-and-half if you want it richer)
steps: First things first — go outside and grab the snow. Use a large clean bowl and scoop from the top layer of a fresh snowfall. You want the fluffy, untouched stuff that just fell — not the packed-down layer, not the stuff near the sidewalk, and definitely not the yellow snow. If the snow has been sitting for a while, brush the top inch off and grab from underneath. Bring it inside and work fast because it starts melting the second it hits your warm kitchen.

In a separate bowl, combine 1 cup of whole milk, 1/2 cup of sugar, and 1 teaspoon of vanilla extract. Stir until the sugar is mostly dissolved — it does not need to be perfect, just give it 30 seconds of good stirring. If you want a richer, creamier result, use half-and-half instead of milk. Some people swear by sweetened condensed milk here — if that is your thing, use about half a can and skip the sugar entirely.

Pour the milk mixture over the bowl of snow. Start folding it together gently with a big spoon or spatula — think of it like folding egg whites into a batter. You are not stirring aggressively. You are gently turning the snow over and through the liquid until it starts to look like soft-serve ice cream. Add more snow a cup at a time if it is too soupy, or a splash more milk if it is too dry and powdery.

Keep folding until you hit the right consistency. You want it to look like thick, creamy soft serve — it should hold its shape on a spoon but still feel light and fluffy. This happens fast, usually 60 to 90 seconds of folding. Do not overmix or it will compact and get icy.

Scoop it into bowls immediately and eat it right away. Snow ice cream does not store well — it is a right-now food. The magic is in eating it the second it comes together. Hand a bowl to everyone in the house and enjoy it standing in the kitchen while the snow is still falling outside. That is the whole experience.

If you want to get fancy, drizzle chocolate syrup on top, add sprinkles, crush some Oreos over it, or stir in a spoonful of peanut butter before serving. Cocoa powder mixed into the milk mixture makes a great chocolate version. But honestly, the original four-ingredient version is perfect the way it is — grandma knew what she was doing.

title: Turkey Bolognese
description: Ground turkey, crushed tomatoes, garlic, and whole wheat pasta. A family favorite that hits the macros right.
steps: Cook pasta according to package directions. Drain and set aside.
Heat olive oil in a large skillet over medium-high heat.
Add diced onion and cook 3-4 minutes until softened.
Add garlic and cook 30 seconds until fragrant.
Add ground turkey, breaking it up with a spoon. Cook until browned, about 6-7 minutes.
Add crushed tomatoes, Italian seasoning, red pepper flakes, salt, and pepper.
Simmer on low for 10-15 minutes to let flavors develop.
Serve over pasta and top with fresh basil.

title: Turkey & Hummus Wrap
description: Whole wheat wrap, sliced turkey, hummus, spinach, cucumber, and a little feta. Packs well for work.
steps: Lay wrap flat and spread hummus evenly across the center.
Layer turkey slices over the hummus.
Add spinach, cucumber slices, and feta.
Fold in the sides, then roll tightly from the bottom.
Cut in half diagonally. Wrap in foil for transport if meal prepping.
